nativeRange.habitat: Rivers, tributaries, and standing waters including pools and small lakes; benthic dweller in shallow, often well-vegetated freshwaters with soft substrates.

diet.notes: Accepts virtually anything offered in captivity. Quality sinking wafers and pellets form the staple, supplemented regularly with frozen or live foods such as bloodworms, tubifex, and brine shrimp. Blanched vegetables are also accepted. Generally considered easy to feed; requires targeted sinking foods as it tends not to thrive on surface-dwelling tankmate leftovers alone.

goodWith: goldfish and temperate-water fish (owing to its cooler-water tolerance)
avoidWith: predatory fish that can swallow them
fish requiring high tropical temperatures above 26°C

summary: A temperate-tolerant Corydoras from the Paraná basin, the Peppered Cory thrives at cooler temperatures than most of its relatives and is one of the most reliably bred catfish in the hobby.
careNotes:
The Peppered Cory is one of the most adaptable and widely kept Corydoras species. Its most distinctive husbandry trait is its tolerance of cooler water: FishBase records wild specimens in the range of 18–23°C, while most aquarium sources suggest keeping it up to about 26°C. This cooler tolerance makes it one of the few Corydoras that can be kept alongside goldfish, fancy goldfish, or other temperate-water species in unheated or lightly heated aquaria — a notably different setup option compared to most tropical Corydoras.

A school of at least 6 is strongly recommended; lone specimens or very small groups tend to become reclusive and stressed. The minimum tank should offer adequate bottom footprint — an 80 cm long tank is a reasonable floor for a group.

Substrate quality is important: smooth sand is strongly preferred over coarse gravel. Rough substrates have been documented to damage the sensitive barbels (whiskers), which can lead to bacterial infection and decline. A sand depth of at least 3 cm is recommended.

Like other Callichthyidae, Corydoras paleatus is a facultative air-breather and will occasionally dart to the surface to gulp air — this is normal behavior and not a distress signal, though persistent surface gulping in well-oxygenated water may indicate gill irritation.

The pectoral fin spines are sharp and can pierce skin; use a soft mesh net and handle carefully. Spines can also snag in fine-mesh nets.

Copper-based medications should be avoided — as with most catfishes, this species has been documented as sensitive to copper at therapeutic doses. Use copper-free alternatives for disease treatment.

Females are noticeably broader and larger-bodied than males, especially when viewed from above when gravid.
breedingNotes: Among the easiest Corydoras to breed in captivity, and one of the most reliable egg-layers in the freshwater hobby. Condition the group well with live or frozen foods (bloodworms, tubifex) for a week or two. Spawning is commonly triggered by a substantial water change (50–70%) using water several degrees cooler than the tank, mimicking the onset of the rainy season — the species' cooler-water tolerance makes this trigger especially effective compared to warmer Corydoras species. A ratio of 2–3 males per female is commonly recommended. Spawning follows the characteristic Corydoras 'T-position': the male grips the female's barbels with his pectoral fins while she cups sperm in her ventral fins and then deposits the adhesive eggs on glass, plants, filter intakes, or other hard surfaces. Clutch sizes can be quite large (commonly 100–300 eggs over a session). Eggs hatch in approximately 3–5 days depending on temperature. Fry begin free-swimming shortly after and can be raised on microworms and baby brine shrimp. Adults may consume eggs if not removed promptly after spawning.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Comprehensive species profile covering size, water parameters, tank requirements, diet, breeding, and taxonomy. Primary source for aquarium husbandry data.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (6.6–7.1 cm SL), temperature range (18–23°C, confirming subtropical/cooler-water nature), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–19 dH), native range (Argentina, Brazil, Uruguay), family Callichthyidae, order Siluriformes. Temperature lower end (18°C) is notably cooler than many aquarium trade sources suggest; applied full cross-source range of 18–26°C.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: General Corydoras care guide. Confirms Peppered Cory is 'on the cooler end of the spectrum,' minimum 20-gallon group tank, group size 6+, diet (sinking wafers, bloodworms), and substrate preferences. Aquarium Co-Op species-specific page returned 404; blog post used instead.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-layer; adhesive eggs deposited on glass, plants, and hard surfaces via Corydoras T-position. Spawning reliably triggered by cool water changes. One of the easiest Corydoras and one of the most reliably bred catfish in the hobby.
